did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used computer vision and machine learning algorithms to diagnose melanoma from dermoscopic images. Specifically, it evaluated results from 25 teams in an international computer vision melanoma challenge and created 'fusion' algorithms using machine learning methods to combine individual automated predictions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper describes a cross-sectional study comparing the diagnostic accuracy of computer algorithms and dermatologists using a specific dataset of 100 dermoscopic images. It reports original experimental results, including sensitivity, specificity, and p-values, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Results of the 2016 International Skin Imaging Collaboration International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging challenge: Comparison of the accuracy of computer algorithms to dermatologists for the diagnosis of melanoma from dermoscopic images
journal: Journal of the American Academy of Dermatology
brief_summary: This study compared the diagnostic accuracy of computer algorithms and dermatologists for melanoma detection using dermoscopic images. The researchers analyzed a subset of 100 images from the 2016 International Skin Imaging Collaboration challenge dataset, which included 50 melanomas, 44 nevi, and 6 lentigines. They evaluated results from 25 individual teams and created 'fusion' algorithms using five methods to combine automated predictions. Eight dermatologists also classified the lesions as benign or malignant for comparison. The average sensitivity and specificity of dermatologists were 82% and 59%, respectively. The best-performing fusion algorithm significantly outperformed dermatologists in specificity (76% vs. 59%, P = .02) at the same sensitivity level.

lesion_types_studied.details: Primary diagnostic target; 50 melanoma images were included in the study subset of 100 images.
Benign comparator; 44 nevus images were included in the study subset.
Benign comparator; 6 lentigines were included in the study subset.

all_task_types.details: The study evaluates computer algorithms and dermatologists for the diagnosis of melanoma from dermoscopic images. The task involves classifying lesions as either benign or malignant. Performance is measured using sensitivity and specificity, and Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curves are reported. The fusion algorithms combine individual automated predictions to produce these binary diagnostic classifications.

datasets_used.name: 2016 International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) Challenge Dataset
datasets_used.description: An international computer vision melanoma challenge dataset containing dermoscopic images. The study utilized a subset of 100 randomly selected images from this larger collection.

use.testing: Used as the test set for evaluating the diagnostic accuracy of 25 individual computer algorithm teams and 8 dermatologists.
use.other: The larger challenge dataset (n=379) served as the source pool from which the 100-image subset was randomly selected.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To compare the specificity of dermatologists against the top challenge algorithm and the best-performing fusion algorithm at a fixed sensitivity level of 82%.
To evaluate and compare the diagnostic accuracy of computer algorithms and dermatologists across different thresholds.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no difference in specificity between the dermatologists and the computer algorithms (top challenge algorithm or best-performing fusion algorithm) at a fixed sensitivity of 82%.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a difference in specificity between the dermatologists and the computer algorithms at a fixed sensitivity of 82%.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: At 82% sensitivity, dermatologist specificity (59%) was statistically similar to the top challenge algorithm (62%, P = .68) but significantly lower than the best-performing fusion algorithm (76%, P = .02).

2016 International Skin Imaging Collabor.notes: At a fixed sensitivity of 82%, dermatologist specificity (59%) was statistically similar to the top challenge algorithm (62%, P = .68) but significantly lower than the best-performing fusion algorithm (76%, P = .02). The fusion algorithms were created using 5 methods (nonlearned and machine learning) to combine individual automated predictions from 25 teams.

architecture_details.note: The paper evaluated results from 25 individual teams in the 2016 International Skin Imaging Collaboration challenge but did not report the specific AI architectures, model families, or technical details of these individual algorithms. It created 'fusion' algorithms using five methods (nonlearned and machine learning) to combine individual automated predictions.

training_details.note: Training details for the individual challenge algorithms were not reported. The fusion algorithms were created by combining predictions from the 25 teams.

classification_or_output_head.note: The fusion algorithms produced binary diagnostic classifications (benign vs. malignant) by combining individual automated predictions.

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Fusion algorithms (created using 5 methods: nonlearned and machine learning)
conclusions: Computer vision algorithms, specifically fusion algorithms combining predictions from multiple teams, can achieve diagnostic accuracy for melanoma detection that is comparable to or exceeds that of dermatologists.
At a fixed sensitivity of 82%, the best-performing fusion algorithm demonstrated significantly higher specificity (76%) compared to the average dermatologist (59%, P = .02).

The top individual challenge algorithm showed specificity (62%) similar to dermatologists (59%, P = .68) at the same sensitivity level, suggesting that combining multiple algorithms via fusion can enhance performance beyond individual state-of-the-art models.
The study supports the potential of computer vision to aid in melanoma detection by providing automated diagnostic support with high specificity.
important_remarks: The study utilized a small, randomly selected subset of 100 images from a larger challenge dataset (n=379), which may limit the generalizability of the findings compared to the full dataset.

The diagnostic accuracy of dermatologists was evaluated in a companion study, meaning the human performance metrics were not derived from the same immediate experimental run as the algorithm results, potentially introducing variability in the comparison.

The top-performing fusion algorithm significantly outperformed dermatologists in specificity (76% vs. 59%, P = .02) at a fixed sensitivity of 82%, highlighting the potential advantage of ensemble methods over individual algorithms.

The top individual challenge algorithm did not significantly outperform dermatologists in specificity (62% vs. 59%, P = .68), suggesting that combining multiple algorithms via fusion is necessary to surpass human expert performance in this context.
Potential conflicts of interest exist, as one author (Dr. Codella) is an employee and stockholder of IBM, which may influence the interpretation of the results regarding IBM-affiliated algorithms.
The study focuses on binary classification (benign vs. malignant) and does not report performance on more complex diagnostic tasks or other skin lesion types beyond melanoma, nevi, and lentigines.
